prompt:
Ticket from the shared-notes squad. Our pad feature does naive last-write-wins on the whole document and people lose paragraphs when two of them type at once. I want the replication core as a plain TypeScript library — no DOM, no network, no async: sites are objects, and 'sending' ops is just calling apply() on another site with a JSON round-trip in between. Runs under node --test with type stripping, so plain annotations only (no enums/namespaces/decorators) and zero npm deps. Tests: test_site.ts + test_converge.ts, already written, `node --test test_site.ts test_converge.ts`, hands off the test files.

Four modules, this exact split, with explicit './x.ts' import specifiers: ids.ts (op identity), doc.ts (the character store + ordering), oplog.ts (seen-set, buffering, per-site log), site.ts (the Site facade tying it together). Tests import { Site } from './site.ts' and { compareIds, idKey } from './ids.ts'.

Identity: every op carries an id { clock, site }. clock is a per-site lamport counter: generating an op increments it; applying (or even buffering) a received op raises it to at least that op's clock, so ops generated after a merge are newer than anything seen. compareIds orders by clock then site, both ascending; idKey renders '<clock>@<site>'. The pair (clock, site) is the total order everything below leans on — later means bigger.

Ops are plain JSON data with EXACTLY these fields (they are the wire format; extra fields are a compatibility break): { kind: 'insert', id, after, char } where after is the id of the character it follows or null for document start and char is a single character; { kind: 'delete', id, target }; { kind: 'restore', id, target }.

Document model — spell this out because merge order must be identical everywhere: conceptually each character is a child of its anchor (the `after` character, or the document root). Children of the same anchor sort by id DESCENDING (newest closest to the anchor); the document is the pre-order walk of that tree. Deleted characters become tombstones: invisible, but they keep their place so later anchors still resolve. Visibility is a last-writer-wins register per character: it starts visible with the character's own id as writer; every delete/restore op targeting it with a LARGER id than the current writer flips it to hidden/visible and becomes the writer; smaller ids lose and do nothing. That's the whole conflict story: concurrent inserts at one spot order by id (site breaks clock ties), concurrent delete-vs-restore goes to the bigger id.

new Site(siteId) — positive integer or RangeError mentioning 'site id'. API, all index math over VISIBLE characters only:
- insert(index, text): Op[] — one insert op per character, chained: first char anchors to the visible char left of index (null at 0), each next char anchors to the previous new one. index > visible length: RangeError 'insert index out of range'. Empty text: returns [] and records nothing.
- delete(index, count = 1): Op[] — one delete op per targeted char (the visible chars at index..index+count-1, in order). Out of range: RangeError 'delete range out of range'. count 0: [].
- undo(): Op[] | null — takes back this site's most recent not-yet-undone LOCAL action, where an action is one insert()/delete() call (multi-char calls undo atomically). Undoing an insert emits delete ops for its chars; undoing a delete emits restore ops; either way in the same order as the original ops, applied locally and returned for shipping. Ops emitted by undo are not themselves undoable (no redo), remote ops are never undone by me, and when every local action is undone it returns null.
- apply(ops): void — deliver ops from anywhere. Idempotent by op id: seen ops (applied OR still buffered) are dropped, so re-delivery storms are harmless. An op whose dependency hasn't arrived (insert whose anchor char is unknown, delete/restore whose target is unknown) is buffered and applied automatically once the dependency lands, cascading. pending(): number of buffered ops.
- text(): the visible string. snapshot(): the full character array in document order INCLUDING tombstones, entries of exactly { id: '<clock>@<site>', char, visible } — two sites that have seen the same ops must produce deep-equal snapshots, tombstones and all. log(): every op this site generated, in generation order (undo ops included).

No timers, no randomness, no Math.random ids — determinism is the deliverable. Green bar on `node --test test_site.ts test_converge.ts` (the converge suite replays every permutation of delivery order and expects byte-identical snapshots) means done.
